notes: Vedic fire oblation ritual. Core Brahmanical practice.
Hindu devotional worship. No Austronesian cognate.
Variant spelling of pūjā.
Sanskrit sacred utterance. Vedic origin.
Sanskrit heaven/paradise. Indra's realm.
Variant spelling of svarga.
Sanskrit cosmic cycle. Puranic cosmology.
Rice ball offering to ancestors. Śrāddha ritual.
Sanskrit 'father/ancestor'. Pitṛloka concept.
Sanskrit cosmic dissolution/destruction.
Sanskrit 'elapsed' — used in Śaka dating formula.
Indian lunar mansion system (27/28 divisions). Imported.
Indian lunar day (1/30 of lunar month). Imported.
Indian Śaka era (78 CE epoch). Imported.
Variant of saka.
Sanskrit weekday. 7-day week imported from India.
Javanese 30-week (210-day) cycle. NO Indian equivalent. Considered indigenous Austronesian. Balinese pawukon still active. KEY FINDING: co-exists with Indic calendar in same inscription.

Old Javanese 'divine/sacred'. Reflex of Proto-Malayo-Polynesian *qiang 'spirit, deity'. Pre-dates Indianization. Found in ALL 10 pilot inscriptions — even Sanskrit-heavy ones use hyaṁ alongside deva/devatā. Survives as modern Javanese 'hyang' (Sang Hyang Widi).
Variant spelling of hyaṁ. Same PMP *qiang etymology.
Old Javanese 'to return/come back (of spirits)'. No Sanskrit source. Possibly related to ancestor spirit return concept. Cf. Malagasy 'famadihana' (turning of the dead). CROSS-AUSTRONESIAN CANDIDATE.
Old Javanese village community term. Related to Balinese 'desa adat/krama'. Indigenous social-ritual organization.
Old Javanese 'purchase/redemption'. Used in ritual context of redeeming land or obligations. No Sanskrit equivalent.

Sanskrit word for 'boundary', but the Javanese sīma ritual (boundary consecration with imprecation) has elements not found in Indian dharmaśāstra. The sapatha (curse) component may be indigenous. Cf. Balinese temple boundary rituals.
Sanskrit 'oath/curse', but the elaborate Javanese imprecation formulae (invoking natural disasters on oath-breakers) go far beyond Indian models. May overlay an indigenous oath tradition.
Variant spelling of śapatha. Same ambiguous classification.
Sanskrit 'ocean', but ocean cosmology is central to Austronesian worldview independently. Could be Sanskrit word covering indigenous concept.
Could be Sanskrit or Austronesian. Cf. Balinese Dewi Danu (lake goddess), possibly pre-Indic water spirit with Sanskrit veneer.
Old Javanese title/honorific in ritual context. Etymology unclear. Possibly hybrid or indigenous.
Sanskrit 'mountain', but sacred mountain concept is pan-Austronesian (cf. Maori Maunga, Balinese Gunung Agung). Sanskrit word may overlay indigenous mountain veneration.
